output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.8, "R1.6": 0.4, "R1.7": 0.95}}

Summary: A critique focused on a likely-fabricated citation, under-specified methods, and a weak comparative baseline.

**1. The AmbigSQL citation is the biggest problem and should be verified before anything else.**
- "AmbigSQL (Chen et al., 2021)" is described as "the canonical resource for evaluating ambiguity handling in text-to-SQL." I cannot confirm such a benchmark exists under that name and year. The actual landscape of ambiguity-aware text-to-SQL benchmarks I'm aware of includes AmbiQT (Bhaskar et al., 2023) and related work on Spider-DK / Spider-Syn — none called "AmbigSQL" from 2021.
- The "v3 release" with very specific properties (4,200 additional queries, a five-way taxonomy with exactly those five categories, ambiguity-weighted execution accuracy, ground-truth clarification annotations) is the kind of claim that is easy to fabricate and hard for a reviewer to catch. Every one of those specifics needs a direct citation to a paper, repo, or release notes.
- If the benchmark is real but mis-named or mis-dated, fix it. If it is not real, the entire experimental section collapses, since the headline metric (73.4% ambiguity-weighted execution accuracy) is defined by that benchmark and the training data is drawn from it.
- Also: the comparison paragraph claims AmbigSQL v3 is "the only benchmark that explicitly labels ambiguity types and includes ground-truth clarification questions." AmbiQT explicitly labels ambiguity types (column/value join). That claim is almost certainly false as written.

**2. The novelty story does not engage with the relevant prior work.**
- "Recent work has reframed text-to-SQL as a two-stage problem" — no citation. Concrete prior systems (AmbiQT, DAMO, interactive SQL work from Yao et al., the "ask-or-answer" literature, clarification-question generation work in general dialog) should be named, and the paper should say what ClarifySQL does that they don't. As written, the contribution reduces to "we fine-tuned Llama-3.1-8B with a clarification head on benchmark X," which is not by itself a contribution.
- The "learned policy for when to ask" is the most interesting-sounding claim and is never specified: is it a threshold on a calibrated classifier, an RL policy, a learned-to-defer head? Without a description, novelty cannot be assessed.

**3. The empirical claims are under-supported.**
- The only baseline is the same model without the clarification head. That isolates the head's contribution but says nothing about whether ClarifySQL is competitive with stronger text-to-SQL systems, including non-clarifying ones (GPT-4-based pipelines, DAIL-SQL, CHESS) or with prior clarification-aware systems. A 6.1-point gain over your own ablation is the floor of what such an architecture should produce, not evidence of state-of-the-art behavior.
- "12% reduction in confidently-wrong SQL generations on production-style ambiguous queries" — "confidently-wrong" is never operationalized (confidence threshold? execution-mismatch? human-judged?), and the "production-style" set is never described (size, provenance, annotation procedure, whether it overlaps with the 4,200 production queries in v3). As written, this number is uninterpretable.
- "Suggesting that the clarification policy generalizes beyond the labeled distribution" overreads a single out-of-distribution number from an undescribed set.
- 8,400 fine-tuning examples is small; report whether you held out a portion of the 8,400 or used the official v3 dev split, and report variance across seeds.

**4. Internal consistency issues.**
- The abstract-like opening says the system "combines schema-grounded ambiguity detection with a learned policy for when to ask," but the methods paragraph only mentions "a lightweight clarification head." Detection vs. policy are presented as one component in one place and two components in another. Clarify whether ambiguity detection and ask-decision share parameters.
- "Fine-tuned on 8,400 v3 examples" vs. the dataset description, which mentions 4,200 additional production queries on top of an unstated base size. State the v3 total size and the train/dev/test split, and reconcile with 8,400.
- The closing sentence frames this as "a step toward production-grade text-to-SQL." The evidence presented (one benchmark dev split, one ablation, one undefined OOD number) does not support a production-grade claim. Either soften the framing or add latency, calibration, and user-study evidence.

**5. Smaller writing issues.**
- "Papering over it with confident generation" is colloquial for a results/discussion sentence; pick one register.
- "Canonical resource" is a strong word for any benchmark less than five years old; downgrade unless you can cite adoption.
- The "top customers" example is good — keep it, but consider also showing the clarification question your system would actually ask, so the reader sees the system's output shape early.

**Concrete recommended next steps before this is reviewable.**
- Confirm AmbigSQL exists, fix the citation (author, year, venue, URL), and link to the v3 release. If it does not exist, the paper needs a different evaluation strategy.
- Add at least one external baseline beyond your own ablation (a strong non-clarifying text-to-SQL baseline and, ideally, a prior clarification-aware system on the same split).
- Define "confidently-wrong" formally and describe the production-style set.
- Specify the clarification policy architecture and training objective in one paragraph.
- Cite AmbiQT and at least two other clarification-aware text-to-SQL systems in the related-work positioning and state the delta.

Uncertainty: My confidence that "AmbigSQL (Chen et al., 2021)" and its "v3 release" are fabricated is high but not absolute — I have not exhaustively searched recent literature, and benchmarks under that name could plausibly exist. The author should verify directly. The characterizations of AmbiQT, BIRD, and Spider are based on my recollection of those works.
